chapters.title: A good team is what shapes a great leader
chapters.text:
The way we think about leadership is changing. Today it is less about one person giving orders and more about helping each teammate lead themselves. Open feedback and shared ownership are slowly replacing the old top down model. Employees are becoming co creators of the work.

Keith Ferrazzi, founder of the research firm Ferrazzi Greenlight, has spent years studying this shift. He shares practical advice on several topics. The first is building teamship. The second is the unwritten agreement between members. The third is turning already talented people into a true super team. The fourth is growing real trust among teammates. The fifth is making collaboration actually produce results. The sixth is drawing introverts into the mix. The seventh is learning to give and take honest feedback.

Great leaders do not stand above their teams. They stand beside them. Every step a company takes toward a more human culture is a step toward a better workplace. You can choose to be that kind of leader and bring the change closer.

The future of leadership belongs to everyone, and your next choice could be the spark that starts it.

chapters.title: What teamship really means
chapters.text:
For decades, companies put the leader at the center of every success story. Ferrazzi first saw the gap as a student at Harvard Business School, and he made it his focus as soon as he graduated. He joined firms like Deloitte and Starwood and pushed them to value team collaboration, which he calls teamship. He points to two main reasons. First, members lift each other up. Second, collaboration beats top down management.

In the early 2000s, the Gallup employee engagement survey, known as the Q12 method, focused only on individuals. Ferrazzi went to a Gallup leader and offered a richer way to measure team performance. He calls co elevation the shared promise to chase a common mission while also watching out for each other.

This matters because avoiding conflict can quietly drain shareholder value and weaken team trust. To co elevate means to speak honestly and treat each other with respect. Ferrazzi says co elevation builds a healthier workplace and saves leaders about thirty percent of their time.

Teamship also streamlines the work itself. Instead of long drawn out meetings, members co create bold ideas. New collaboration tools and AI are reshaping how engineers and teammates work. Peers co lead the work rather than taking orders from the top.

A good first step is to ask where your team stands today. When the team senses untapped potential, it is time to address the hidden conflicts and unspoken criticism. This calls for deliberate bond building, especially for hybrid teams whose members rarely meet in person. Caring for common success means caring for each member too. Stronger bonds build resilience and invite broader co creation. Recognizing one another naturally shifts the culture from leader led praise to peer celebration. This approach supports collaboration and mutual coaching.

chapters.title: Hire stars and make a constellation
chapters.text:
Moving from leader led work to team co elevation requires candor and trust. Sergey Young, who helped create the XPRIZE Healthspan competition aimed at extending healthy human life, wanted to focus on other projects while leaving his team self managing. Candor and co elevation cut down on isolated work, encouraged people to speak up, and raised shared accountability. Here is how it worked.

If a leader hits a ceiling, the team does too. Candor and mutual support form the social contract that holds every team together. You may have heard the phrase "meeting after the meeting," when a few close colleagues talk about the real problems they hesitated to raise out loud. That kind of hidden critique, along with personal grudges, slowly weakens the social contract.

People stay quiet for different reasons. The most common is fear of hurting a friend or colleague. Others are simply passive or feel powerless. Yet naming a missed detail can surface blind spots and spark fresh thinking. The challenge is doing it in a way that feels safe.

Ferrazzi offers six practices to break the ice. The first is the Power of Three, where you split the meeting into groups of three to dig into nagging issues, then report back and find a common fix. A shared document helps. The second is Stress Testing, where instead of a normal success report, each person gives a one slide summary of struggles and plans for the next phase, and the group works together on solutions. The third is the Candor Break, where the leader pauses the meeting and asks what is not being said that should be. A shared document helps here too. The fourth is Outlier Meetings, where you pick critical growth areas and keep them on the list until the team solves them. The fifth is Yoda in the Room, where a few trusted teammates can stop the meeting with a safe word when they sense a real issue is being avoided. The sixth is Hiring Candor, where you are upfront about the job challenges so the candidate can decide if they are a good fit.

Ferrazzi found that small breakout rooms raise candor by about eighty five percent, because people feel less nervous talking in a tight group.

chapters.title: Your business becomes everyone is business
chapters.text:
A solid team is made of professionals working toward a shared goal. A great team is made of people who share more than just the work. How well we do our jobs depends on what happens in our lives and how we feel. The same things can also strengthen the team through mutual understanding.

Ferrazzi names three kinds of trust. The first is professional trust. The second is structural trust. The third is personal trust.

Professional trust is simple. People count on each other is competence. Structural trust means teammates can hold different professional views no matter how warm the friendship. Personal trust can transform the team climate. It used to build itself through office life, but hybrid work has changed that. People form fewer chance bonds, so teammates stay loosely connected.

Hard feedback goes down easier when it comes from a friend. Ferrazzi remembers a union leader at his first job in a manufacturing plant who walked up to the new manager, praised his work ethic, and then said they should go have a drink. The formula works in two steps. First, recognize the other person is contribution and build professional trust. Then invite them to coffee or lunch to get to know them better.

A quarterly intimacy dinner can warm the room and pull teammates into each other is lives. Sharing a meal builds trust and deepens personal bonds. For everyday bonding, two short activities work well. The first is Sweet and Sour, where the team spends five minutes at the start of a meeting sharing something good and something hard from their life. The second is Personal Professional Check ins, where you ask sincerely what is really going on and whether the person wants to talk. This one is longer and more personal.

It also helps to skip toxic positivity. Your best today may not be your best tomorrow. Teammates should feel safe to share feelings and struggles. That is the heart of personal trust.

chapters.title: Meetings are not the same as collaboration
chapters.text:
Old companies leaned heavily on team leaders as the engine of progress. Today the model is shifting toward employee ideas and joint work.

Asynchronous collaboration pulls employees in by letting them work toward the same goal at different times and places. It leans on shared documents and online tools rather than long meetings that drained remote and office workers alike.

A good rule is to ask if your meeting could have been an email. Asynchronous work answers that by giving everyone a shared document to discuss issues and suggest fixes. People can take their time, share ideas, and develop a few directions to talk through later. It saves time and draws in voices that usually stay quiet, including introverts whose views matter as much as anyone is. Asynchronous collaboration also helps with the team is direction, goals, and challenges, and it clears the calendar of meetings that should never have been there, which Ferrazzi calls calendar bankruptcy.

Another idea worth knowing is radical adaptability. It encourages crowdsourcing ideas between teams through async work and using generative AI to test bolder team or company moves. For example, AI can build personas to pressure test new marketing ideas, saving the cost of polling real customers. Ferrazzi believes humans and AI will soon be teammates, with AI amplifying our work. Beyond time sucking tasks, AI can handle scheduling and email, which frees people for collaboration and creative thinking.

In short, async collaboration and radical adaptability strengthen teamship by helping teams work with other teams, breaking complex projects into doable chunks, stress testing ideas with the customer in mind, sorting urgent from important work, and tracking each person is contribution and current focus. AI can sharpen your problem solving if you are ready to share a piece of the decision making.

chapters.title: The power of being seen
chapters.text:
AI has earned its place in the workforce, but it cannot replace human connection. Ferrazzi is research shows that more than half of employees leave their jobs because they feel unrecognized. Working without acknowledgment or purpose wears people down. Work is unavoidable, so it helps to value the time and effort we put in.

Teams suffer when their leader burns out. If you lead a team, take a hard look at your self talk and your habits. Carol Clements, chief digital officer at JetBlue, made it her mission to recognize her employees is work and invite them to recognize each other. She asks two questions during onboarding. What motivates you. And how do you want to be recognized.

Praise matters for every teammate, including those who are underperforming, since the right kind of recognition often lifts them up. Every situation is different, but nobody does poor work on purpose. They usually do not see why their work misses the mark. That is why knowing what drives your teammates, or what drives you as a teammate, is so important.

In a world of global business and global clients, we all need to try seeing life through other people is eyes.

No matter your role, highlighting each other is wins can lift the room. One method is seeding appreciation, where a leader passes praise to a teammate through friends or even parents, and everyone benefits. Another is the gratitude circle, where after each meeting the team reflects silently and then gives each person a minute or two to share a win or thanks.

Ferrazzi also values inclusion and diversity. Teammates feel more at ease when they are accepted, and the team often makes better calls. Pool Knox from Frito Lay shared how the team invented a popular Jalapeño chip flavor by listening to Latin American employees in Texas, who knew their customers is tastes and helped scientists land on a winner.

If diversity drives profit, why is it still underused.

chapters.title: Turn a colleague into a friend
chapters.text:
Where there is diversity, there must also be support and honest feedback. The word candor has been popping up in team talks, so it is worth a closer look.

Honesty always leads to raw insight. What you do with it matters most. Sugarcoating or skipping feedback can make someone think they are doing fine. In reality, they miss chances to grow and risk standing still, dragging the team with them. This applies to senior people as much as to newcomers.

Some who step into leadership assume they do not need peer coaching. But how can they make tough calls without enough feedback. On top of that, many professionals stop developing themselves after a promotion, which dulls their skills and their connection with the team.

Junior and mid level teammates need regular feedback to know where to grow. Have you ever wanted to level up at work but found no time because of your workload. Without steady feedback, the work gets harder, which makes the workload heavier, a cycle that is hard to break.

Candid but encouraging feedback clears many bottlenecks in productivity and motivation. Three practices help. The first is Open 360, a quarterly session where each teammate shares one praise and one growth area for a peer, and the leader makes sure old grudges are not simmering. The second is Dial Up and Dial Down, a self reflection exercise where each person lists what to do more of and what to do less of, then makes a visual reminder like a sticker to stay on track. The third is the 5/5/5 Learning Roadmap, where each person walks through their strengths, weaknesses, and growth plans, spends five minutes updating peers, five minutes answering questions, and five minutes receiving feedback.

Nothing beats real friendship, though. Flex CEO Revathi Advaithi believes time together outside the office is vital for teamship. Her way of connecting is simple. She likes to know people as humans, not just as colleagues.

chapters.title: A few things to try
chapters.text:
Moving from leadership to teamship is about unlocking the group is collective power through collaboration, trust, and shared responsibility. Every small step you take to build a stronger team brings you closer to a culture where everyone can thrive.

A weekly team huddle works well. Set aside fifteen minutes each week for an open talk where each person shares one challenge and one way the team can help. You can also create a no blame culture by introducing a shared line about mistakes being learning moments, and modeling it yourself when things go wrong. Cross collaboration is another lever, so pair two teammates who rarely work together on a small project to build fresh bonds and new views. Ask for feedback often and try each teamship practice to see what sticks. Rotating leadership roles is also powerful, so let each teammate lead a meeting or a project over the next three months to build confidence and shared accountability.

Your path to true teamship starts now, and taking action together will lift the whole team.
